<think>
The user wants a final research report on independent directors under NASDAQ listing standards. I have five panel answers and judge analysis to guide me. Let me synthesize the best elements:

1. Structure from Model 3 or 5 (clear tables and bullet points)
2. Two-part test definition
3. Six bright-line disqualifiers with quantitative thresholds
4. Specific exemptions (Controlled Companies, FPIs, etc.)
5. SEC Rule 10A-3 for audit committees
6. Note that stock ownership alone doesn't preclude independence
7. Phase-in schedules and cure periods

Let me write a comprehensive, well-structured final report.
</think>
# Independent Directors Under NASDAQ Listing Standards

## 1. Definition of an Independent Director

Under **NASDAQ Listing Rule 5605(a)(2)**, an "Independent Director" is defined as:

> "A person other than an Executive Officer or employee of the Company or any other individual having a relationship which, in the opinion of the Company's board of directors, would interfere with the exercise of independent judgment in carrying out the responsibilities of a director."

This definition operates as a **two-part test**:

- **Part 1 — Affirmative Board Determination (Principles-Based):** The board must affirmatively determine that the director has no relationship that would interfere with the exercise of independent judgment.
- **Part 2 — Bright-Line Disqualifiers (Rule-Based):** The director must not fall within any of the specific objective categories enumerated in paragraphs (A)–(F) of the rule (or, for investment companies, paragraph (G)).

For purposes of the rule, **"Family Member"** is defined broadly to include a person's spouse, parents, children, siblings, mothers- and fathers-in-law, sons- and daughters-in-law, brothers- and sisters-in-law, and anyone (other than domestic employees) who shares such person's home.

**Notable point:** NASDAQ specifically states that ownership of company stock, by itself, does **not** preclude a board finding of independence.

---

## 2. Eligibility Criteria (Who Qualifies)

To qualify as an independent director, a person must satisfy **all** of the following:

1. **Not be an Executive Officer or employee** of the company (currently, nor within the past three years, with limited exceptions for interim executive officer service of less than one year).
2. **No disqualifying relationship** under any of the six bright-line tests described below.
3. **No other relationship** that, in the board's opinion, would interfere with the exercise of independent judgment — the board must make and document an affirmative determination of independence.
4. The independence determination must be publicly disclosed (under Item 407(a) of Regulation S-K for U.S. domestic companies; in Form 20-F or 40-F for Foreign Private Issuers).

For service on the **audit committee**, the director must additionally satisfy the more stringent **SEC Rule 10A-3(b)(1)** independence requirements.

---

## 3. Disqualification Criteria (Who Cannot Serve)

Under **Rule 5605(a)(2)(A)–(F)**, the following persons **shall not** be considered independent. All tests use a **three-year look-back period** unless otherwise noted.

| # | Disqualifying Relationship | Key Threshold / Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | **Employment by the Company** — A director who is, or at any time during the past three years was, employed by the Company or any of its subsidiaries. | Exception: Interim employment as an Executive Officer for less than one year does not permanently disqualify, but the director is **not** independent while serving as interim officer. |
| **B** | **Excessive Compensation** — A director (or Family Member) who accepted more than **$120,000** in compensation from the Company during any 12-consecutive-month period within the past three years. | Excluded: (i) board/committee fees; (ii) compensation paid to a Family Member who is a non-executive employee; (iii) tax-qualified retirement benefits and non-discretionary compensation. |
| **C** | **Family Member as Executive Officer** — A director who has a Family Member who is, or at any time during the past three years was, employed by the Company as an Executive Officer. | |
| **D** | **Significant Organizational Payments** — A director (or Family Member) who is a partner in, controlling shareholder of, or Executive Officer of any organization to which the Company made, or from which the Company received, payments for property or services in the current or any of the past three fiscal years exceeding the **greater of 5% of the recipient's consolidated gross revenues for that year, or $200,000**. | Excluded: payments arising solely from investments in the Company's securities; payments under non-discretionary charitable contribution matching programs. |
| **E** | **Compensation Committee Interlock** — A director (or Family Member) who is, or has been within the past three years, employed as an Executive Officer of another entity where any of the Company's Executive Officers serve/served on that entity's compensation committee. | |
| **F** | **Auditor Affiliation** — A director (or Family Member) who is a current partner of the Company's outside auditor, or was a partner or employee of the Company's outside auditor who worked on the Company's audit at any time during the past three years. | |

**Paragraph (G) — Investment Companies:** For a fund registered under the Investment Company Act of 1940, paragraphs (A)–(F) are replaced by the "interested person" test of Section 2(a)(19) of the 1940 Act. A director who is an "interested person" of the company (other than in his or her capacity as a member of the board or any board committee) is not independent.

**Stricter Standards for Audit Committee Members (Rule 5605(c)):** Audit committee members must additionally:
- Meet **SEC Rule 10A-3(b)(1)** criteria (no direct or indirect compensatory fees other than director/committee fees; not an "affiliated person" of the company);
- Not have participated in the preparation of the Company's financial statements at any time during the past three years;
- Be able to read and understand fundamental financial statements (at least one member must have past employment experience in finance or accounting, or comparable financial sophistication).

---

## 4. Which Companies Are Required to Have Independent Directors

### A. General Rule

Under **Rule 5605(b)(1)**, a **majority of the board of directors** of every NASDAQ-listed company (Global Select, Global, and Capital Markets tiers) must be comprised of Independent Directors. This applies to **all** companies with common stock or voting preferred stock listed on NASDAQ, with the specific exemptions noted below.

In addition, the following committee-specific independent-director requirements apply:

- **Audit Committee (Rule 5605(c)):** At least **three** members, each Independent and meeting SEC Rule 10A-3.
- **Compensation Committee (Rule 5605(d)):** At least **two** members, each Independent.
- **Director Nominations (Rule 5605(e)):** Nominations must be made either by a nominations committee composed solely of Independent Directors, or by Independent Directors constituting a majority of the board.

### B. Exemptions Under Rule 5615

The following categories of NASDAQ-listed companies are exempt (in whole or in part) from the independent-director requirements:

| Exempt Category | Rule Ref. | Scope of Exemption |
|---|---|---|
| **Controlled Companies** (>50% voting power held by an individual, group, or other company) | 5615(c)(2) | Exempt from majority-independent-board (5605(b)), compensation committee (5605(d)), and nominations (5605(e)) requirements. **Must still** maintain an independent audit committee and hold executive sessions of independent directors. |
| **Foreign Private Issuers** | 5615(a)(3) | May follow home-country governance practice in lieu of the Rule 5600 Series, provided they disclose deviations in their annual report (Form 20-F or 40-F) and submit a written statement from independent home-country counsel. **Must still** comply with audit-committee independence (Rule 10A-3). |
| **Management Investment Companies** (1940 Act Funds) | 5615(a)(5) | Exempt from independent-board (5605(b)), compensation committee (5605(d)), nominations (5605(e)), and code-of-conduct (Rule 5610) requirements. Subject to audit-committee rules under Rule 10A-3. |
| **Asset-Backed / Other Passive Issuers** (e.g., unit investment trusts) | 5615(a)(1) | Exempt from majority-independent-board, audit committee, compensation committee, nominations, and code-of-conduct requirements. |
| **Issuers of Non-Voting Preferred Securities, Debt Securities, or Derivative Securities Only** | 5615(a)(6) | Exempt from independent-board, compensation committee, nominations, code-of-conduct, and shareholder-meeting requirements. Subject only to SEC Rule 10A-3 audit-committee requirements. If the issuer also lists common or voting preferred stock, the full Rule 5600 Series applies. |
| **Limited Partnerships** | 5615(a)(4) | Generally not subject to the Rule 5600 Series (except as otherwise provided). |
| **Cooperatives** | 5615(a)(2) | Exempt from certain corporate governance requirements. |

### C. Phase-In Schedules (Rule 5615(b))

Newly listing companies are granted phase-in periods:

- **IPO Listings:** 12 months from the Listing Date to achieve a majority-independent board; audit committee must have at least one independent member at listing, a majority within 90 days, and full compliance within one year; compensation and nominations committees phased in on the same schedule.
- **Companies Emerging from Bankruptcy, Transferring from Other Markets, or Listing via Spin-Off/Carve-Out:** Receive similar phase-in treatment under Rules 5615(b)(2)–(b)(5).

### D. Cure Periods (Rule 5605(b)(1)(A))

If a company falls below a majority-independent board due to one vacancy or one director ceasing to be independent through circumstances beyond that director's reasonable control, the company regains compliance by the **earlier of its next annual shareholders' meeting or one year** from the triggering event (or 180 days if the annual meeting falls within that window).

---

## Summary

| Element | Key Point |
|---|---|
| **Definition (Rule 5605(a)(2))** | A person other than an Executive Officer or employee of the company, whom the board affirmatively determines has no relationship interfering with independent judgment, and who passes all six bright-line tests (or the Section 2(a)(19) "interested person" test for 1940 Act funds). |
| **Eligibility** | Not currently employed by the company; no family-member executive officer relationship; no excessive compensation (>$120,000 in any 12-month period within 3 years); no significant business relationships; no audit firm ties; no compensation-committee interlocks; and affirmative board determination. |
| **Disqualification** | Six bright-line tests, all with a 3-year look-back: (A) employment by company; (B) compensation >$120,000; (C) family member as executive officer; (D) business payments exceeding the greater of 5% of revenues or $200,000; (E) compensation-committee interlock; (F) auditor affiliation. Investment companies subject to 1940 Act "interested person" test. |
| **Companies Required to Have Independent Directors** | All NASDAQ-listed companies with common or voting preferred stock must maintain a **majority-independent board** and fully independent **audit, compensation, and nominations** committees. Exemptions apply to **controlled companies, foreign private issuers, management investment companies, asset-backed/passive issuers, limited partnerships, cooperatives, and issuers listing only non-voting preferred/debt/derivative securities**. Newly listing companies receive phase-in periods, and cure periods apply for sudden loss of independence. |

Organizes content with clear sections for definition, disqualifications, eligibility, and company requirements
Maintains objective regulatory tone appropriate for compliance documentation without advisory recommendations beyond rule requirements
Uses casual expressions such as "basically", "get a pass", "who's in/out", contractions, or colloquial phrasing
Restates the same disqualification criterion or exemption multiple times using different wording without adding new regulatory detail
Cites NASDAQ Listing Rule 5605(a)(2) for independent director definition and disqualification criteria
Cites NASDAQ Listing Rule 5605(b)(1) for majority independent board requirement
Cites SEC Rule 10A-3 for audit committee independence requirements
Cites NASDAQ Listing Rule 5615 or IM-5605 for exemptions or interpretive guidance
Relies exclusively on legal blogs, law firm marketing materials, or Wikipedia without citing primary NASDAQ rules or SEC regulations

<think>The user wants a final report on Delta ranges for a Collar strategy. I need to synthesize the panel answers and judge analysis into a clean, well-structured response that directly answers all five research questions in the required output format.

Key consensus from panels:
- Sold Call Delta: 0.20-0.35 (sweet spot 0.30)
- Bought Put Delta: -0.20 to -0.30 (sweet spot -0.25)
- Asymmetric is the norm due to volatility skew
- CBOE CLL uses 5% OTM put / 10% OTM call (net debit)
- JPM uses put-spread collar (5% long / 20% short / 3-5% call) - zero-cost
- Position delta typically +0.40 to +0.60

I need to format this cleanly with the required output block and 3+ sources.Let me write a comprehensive but well-organized final report.</think>

# Delta Ranges for Protective Collar Strategy

## Summary Answer

```
Sold Call Delta: 0.20 to 0.35 (sweet spot: 0.30)
  Reason: 0.30 delta is the covered-call industry standard — ~30% probability
  of assignment, 3-5% OTM, and generates enough premium to fund the put. In
  a zero-cost collar, the call often trends to 0.30-0.35 because put skew
  forces it closer to ATM to match premiums. Below 0.20 → premium too thin
  to fund protection. Above 0.40 → upside cap too aggressive.

Bought Put Delta: -0.20 to -0.35 (sweet spot: -0.25 to -0.30)
  Reason: Corresponds to 5-10% OTM, the standard protective-put range. -0.30
  delta (~5% OTM) provides tight protection but is expensive due to skew.
  -0.20 delta (~8-10% OTM) is cheaper and more easily funded by the call
  premium. In a zero-cost collar, the put typically sits at -0.20 to -0.25.

Symmetric vs Asymmetric: ASYMMETRIC (premium-neutral) is the practical
  standard. Due to volatility skew (the "smirk"), OTM puts are systematically
  more expensive than equidistant OTM calls. A truly zero-cost collar almost
  always requires selling the call CLOSER to ATM (higher delta) than the put
  is below the market (lower delta). Symmetric/equidistant collars produce a
  net debit of ~1.3% per year in structural negative alpha. Institutional
  users (CBOE CLL, JPMorgan JHEQX, LGIM) all use asymmetric, premium-matched
  structures.

Example Collar (Zero-Cost, Asymmetric — 30-45 DTE, ~25-30% IV):
  Stock:        $100.00
  Sell call:    $105 strike (+5% OTM), Delta ~0.30, Premium ~$2.20 received
  Buy put:      $92 strike  (-8% OTM), Delta ~-0.20, Premium ~$2.20 paid
  Net credit/debit: $0.00 (zero-cost)
  Max profit:   $500 per contract  ($5 cap × 100 shares)
  Max loss:     $800 per contract  ($8 floor × 100 shares)
  Net position delta: ~+0.50  (1.00 stock − 0.30 call − 0.20 put)

  Alternative — CBOE CLL institutional benchmark (net debit, more upside):
    Buy put $95 (-5% OTM, delta ~-0.27)
    Sell call $110 (+10% OTM, delta ~0.09)
    Net DEBIT ~$1.60/share; position beta ≈ 0.64 vs. S&P 500

  Alternative — JPM Hedged Equity put-spread collar (zero-cost institutional):
    Buy put $95  (-5% OTM, delta ~0.30)
    Sell put $80 (-20% OTM, delta ~0.06)
    Sell call $103 (+3% OTM, delta ~0.35)
    Net: ~$0.00; protects between −5% and −20%; caps upside at ~3%

Sources:
  [1] CBOE — S&P 500 95-110 Collar Index (CLL) Methodology
      https://cdn.cboe.com/resources/indices/documents/newcollardescriptionoct1909.pdf
  [2] AQR Capital Management — "Risk and Return of Equity Index Collar Strategies"
      (Israelov & Klein, 2016 — quantifies skew drag at ~1.3% annually)
      https://www.aqr.com/-/media/AQR/Documents/Journal-Articles/Risk-and-Return.pdf
  [3] SpotGamma — JPMorgan Hedged Equity Collar Trade Breakdown
      (~$22B AUM, 5% long / 20% short put spread + 3-5% short call)
      https://spotgamma.com/jpm-morgan-collar-trade/
  [4] CBOE — Benchmark Indices Series: PPUT, CLL, CLLZ
      https://www.cboe.com/insights/posts/benchmark-indices-series-hedging-downside-exposure-with-pput-cll-and-cllz-indices/
  [5] Ryan O'Connell, CFA — "Collar Option Strategy: Protection with Income"
      https://ryanoconnellfinance.com/collar-strategy/
  [6] OptionVisualizer — Collar Strategy Documentation (delta-screener guidance)
      https://www.optionvisualizer.com/documentation/strategies/collar-strategy
  [7] OptionsDecay — Collar Strategy (Greeks, asymmetry, zero-cost mechanics)
      https://www.optionsdecay.com/strategies/intermediate/collar/
  [8] LGIM America — "Tailor Your Risk with an Equity Collar" (pension-fund paper)
      https://am.landg.us.com/asset/4994de/globalassets/lgima/insights/whitepapers/tailor-your-risk-by-buttoning-up-with-an-equity-collar.pdf
  [9] Investopedia — Zero-Cost Collar (worked example)
      https://www.investopedia.com/terms/z/zerocostcollar.asp
  [10] Executive Comp Advisors — Concentrated Stock Hedging (IRC §1259 spread rules)
      https://executive-comp-advisors.com/hedging-concentrated-stock/
```

---

## 1. Sold Call Delta: 0.20 – 0.35

The covered-call literature is remarkably consistent: **0.30 delta is the single most commonly recommended value**, popularized by TastyTrade, BornToSell, and most options education platforms as the "balanced" default. A 0.30 delta call means roughly a 30% probability of assignment and 70% probability of expiring worthless. At 30 DTE on a $100 stock, this typically corresponds to a strike 3–5% OTM.

| Delta | Assignment Prob | Typical Distance OTM | Use Case |
|-------|----------------|----------------------|----------|
| 0.20 | ~20% | 6–10% OTM | Conservative; more upside room, less premium |
| **0.30** | **~30%** | **3–5% OTM** | **Balanced (most popular); standard collar call** |
| 0.35 | ~35% | 2–4% OTM | Aggressive; more premium to fund tighter put |

In a **collar** specifically, the call delta often trends to the higher end (0.30–0.35) because the call premium must fund a put whose price is inflated by volatility skew. Above 0.40 delta, the call sits so close to the money that the strategy stops being a collar and starts resembling a synthetic short straddle — the upside cap becomes punitive. Below 0.15, the premium is too thin to meaningfully offset the put cost.

---

## 2. Bought Put Delta: −0.20 to −0.35

The protective-put literature converges on **5–10% OTM** as the standard strike selection, corresponding to approximately **0.20–0.30 delta** for 30–90 DTE options under moderate implied volatility.

| Put Delta (abs) | Protection Level | Typical Distance OTM | Cost Profile |
|-----------------|-----------------|----------------------|--------------|
| 0.30–0.40 | Tight (5% OTM) | 3–5% below | Expensive; hard to fund with call alone |
| **0.20–0.30** | **Moderate (5–10% OTM)** | **5–10% below** | **Standard collar range; fundable** |
| 0.10–0.15 | Catastrophe only | 10–15% below | Cheap; minimal protection |

In a **zero-cost collar**, the put delta typically sits at the lower end (0.20–0.25) because put skew makes OTM puts expensive relative to equidistant calls — to achieve premium neutrality, the put must sit further OTM than the call's distance above spot.

---

## 3. Symmetric vs. Asymmetric: **Asymmetric is the norm**

Most traders do **not** aim for equal deltas. The **volatility skew (the "smirk")** forces structural asymmetry:

- OTM puts almost always trade at higher implied volatility than equidistant OTM calls because the market prices downside risk more aggressively.
- To achieve a true zero-cost collar, the call strike must be *closer to the money* (higher delta) than the put strike is below spot (lower delta).
- A symmetric (e.g., 5% OTM call / 5% OTM put) collar almost always has a **net debit** because of skew — AQR research estimates this costs roughly **1.3% per year** in structural negative alpha.
- Visually "symmetric deltas of ~0.30" is retail shorthand; institutional books routinely use **put delta = −0.27 with call delta = 0.09** (CBOE CLL) or higher call delta (~0.35) paired with a −0.20 to −0.30 put to force zero cost.

### Typical Asymmetric Structures

| Collar Type | Call Delta | Put Delta (abs) | Net Position Delta | When to Use |
|-------------|------------|-----------------|--------------------|-------------|
| Zero-Cost (standard) | 0.30–0.35 | 0.20–0.25 | ~+0.45–0.55 | Default; call closer to ATM offsets skew |
| Balanced (textbook) | 0.25–0.30 | 0.25–0.30 | ~+0.50–0.60 | When IV skew is flat; expect small net debit |
| Protective (defensive) | 0.15–0.20 | 0.30–0.40 | ~+0.45–0.60 | When downside protection is the priority |
| Aggressive Income | 0.35–0.40 | 0.10–0.15 | ~+0.40–0.55 | When net credit and wider downside exposure are acceptable |

**Executive hedging tax note:** For concentrated stock positions, IRC §1259 constructive-sale rules require a **30–40% spread** between put and call strikes to avoid adverse tax treatment — forcing a wide, asymmetric structure regardless of skew.

---

## 4. Percentage Distance from Current Price

Delta-based selection is generally preferred over fixed percentages because it **self-adjusts for volatility**: a 0.30 delta call on a low-IV stock might be 3% OTM, while the same delta on a high-IV stock might be 8% OTM. However, percentage benchmarks remain useful reference points:

| Source / Style | Put Distance OTM | Call Distance OTM | Net Cost |
|----------------|-----------------|-------------------|----------|
| CBOE CLL Index (benchmark) | 5% (quarterly) | 10% (monthly) | Net debit |
| CBOE CLLZ (put-spread collar) | 2.5–5% put spread | Dynamic (premium-matched) | Zero-cost |
| JPMorgan JHEQX | 5% long / 20% short | 3–5% | Zero-cost |
| Retail "Balanced" | 5–10% | 5–10% | Slight debit |
| Retail "Tight" | 2–5% | 2–3% | Net debit |
| Retail "Wide / Income" | 8–10% | 3–5% | Net credit possible |
| Dutch pension funds | 10% | 10% | Zero-cost in low-vol regimes |

---

## 5. Institutional Collar Structures

### JPMorgan Hedged Equity Fund (JHEQX) — ~$22B AUM
The single largest systematic options structure in U.S. equities (~35,000 SPX contracts per leg, quarterly reset). Uses a **three-leg put-spread collar**:

| Leg | Strike | Approx. Delta | Role |
|-----|--------|---------------|------|
| Long put | 5% OTM | ~0.30 | Downside protection begins |
| Short put | 20% OTM | ~0.05–0.07 | Funds the long put; caps protection at −20% |
| Short call | 3–5% OTM | ~0.30–0.35 | Funds the put spread; caps upside at ~3–5% |

This is **structurally asymmetric** — the call delta is much higher than the long-put delta because the sold 20%-OTM put absorbs most of the put cost.

### CBOE S&P 500 95-110 Collar Index (CLL)
The passive benchmark since 1986:
- **Long put:** 5% OTM, quarterly expiration (delta ~−0.27)
- **Short call:** 10% OTM, monthly expiration (delta ~0.09)
- **NOT zero-cost:** AQR found it spends ~$7.50 on puts for every $1 collected from calls; position beta ≈ 0.64 vs. S&P 500.

### CBOE CLLZ (Zero-Cost Put Spread Collar Index)
Institutional variant that adds a sold 5% OTM put to fund a tighter 2.5% OTM bought put — trades catastrophic-tail protection for cheaper near-the-money protection.

### LGIM (Pension Funds)
Uses collars during de-risking glidepaths: as funded status improves, equity collar strategies become advantageous for plans transitioning from equities to fixed income. The premium from selling upside calls funds downside puts, effectively reducing equity beta while locking in gains.

### Key Institutional Insight
Pension and endowment collars tend to be **longer-dated (6–18 months)**, **asymmetric in delta** (call delta often higher than put delta magnitude to force zero cost), and frequently use **put spreads** instead of naked puts to control cost.

---

## Practical Notes

- **IV regime matters:** In low-VIX environments (VIX < 15), both deltas can be achieved at wider percentage distances; in high-VIX environments (VIX > 30), the same deltas sit much closer to spot, so strikes compress.
- **Time decay asymmetry:** The short call (positive theta) typically dominates the long put's negative theta, so most zero-cost collars are mildly positive theta — you are paid to hold the hedge, which is part of why institutional holders love them.
- **Roll frequency:** Retail 30–45 DTE is standard; institutional books typically use 3-month puts and 1-month calls, rolling independently.

2. Include specific legal milestones, cases, and scholars
3. Use a comparative structure addressing property, bodily autonomy, and political participation
4. Critically examine "tradition" as an evolving construct
5. Acknowledge intersections between legal-system structure and feminist methodology
