# Trust-Building Mechanisms in Latin American Gig Economy Markets: A Comparative Analysis of Argentina, Brazil, and Colombia

## Executive Summary

Expanding a gig platform into Argentina, Brazil, and Colombia requires trust architecture fundamentally different from U.S. or European models. In these markets, where 43–55% of workers operate in the informal economy, institutional trust in financial and legal systems is historically low, and cash remains the dominant settlement medium. The platforms that succeed design around three structural realities: (1) layered rather than monolithic verification flows; (2) payment escrow built on local instant-payment rails (Pix in Brazil, Mercado Pago in Argentina, Bre-B in Colombia) rather than card-network infrastructure; and (3) dispute resolution with explicit human escalation paths. This report synthesizes evidence from Fairwork assessments, platform documentation, regulatory analysis, and academic research to provide actionable UX guidance for market entry.

---

## 1. Market Context: Why Informality Reshapes Trust Design

The structural variable shaping platform behavior in these markets is informal-economy participation. Argentina's overall informality rate stood at 43% in Q4 2025 (https://www.batimes.com.ar/news/economy/four-in-ten-workers-in-argentina-are-informally-employed-uba-report-finds.phtml), Brazil's at 37.5–39% (https://agenciadenoticias.ibge.gov.br/en/agencia-press-room/2185-news-agency/releases-en/46244-continuous-pnad-unemployment-rate-is-5-8-underutilization-rate-is-14-1-in-quarter-ending-in-february), and Colombia's at 55.1% nationally (https://www.dane.gov.co/index.php/estadisticas-por-tema/mercado-laboral/empleo-informal-y-seguridad-social). Among Brazilian app-based workers specifically, the informality rate is 71.1% (https://www.metropoles.com/brasil/ibge-711-dos-trabalhadores-por-aplicativos-estao-na-informalidade), nearly double the general rate.

The implication for UX is direct: a large share of new users in these markets arrive without a credit history, often without a formal bank account, and with cultural priors that cash is the only "real" settlement. Fairwork's regional assessments confirm that platform fairness remains poor—Argentinian platforms scored 0–4 out of 10 in 2022 (https://fair.work/en/fw/blog/fairwork-argentina-scores-2022-new-report-about-gig-economies-in-latinoamerica/), and in Colombia 2025 only two of five major platforms earned any points (https://urosario.edu.co/periodico-nova-et-vetera/nuestra-u/fairwork-colombia-2025-revela-precariedad-persistente-en-el-trabajo-en-plataformas). In Brazil 2025, only InDrive and Superprof received any points (1 point each) out of 10 (https://olhardigital.com.br/2025/09/23/pro/relatorio-mostra-precariedade-no-trabalho-por-aplicativos-no-brasil/).

---

## 2. Rating Systems: From Star Averages to Algorithmic Scores

### 2.1 The Shared Baseline

All three countries inherit the eBay/Mercado Libre template: post-transaction 1–5 star bilateral ratings, with negative ratings carrying disproportionate weight. Mercado Libre's published Argentina (MLA) reputational logic evaluates the last 60 days once a seller passes 50 sales, and otherwise falls back to 365 days of history (https://developers.mercadolibre.com.ar/es_ar/como-empezar/reputacion-de-vendedores). A study of Buenos Aires-based Mercado Libre transactions estimated a 0.8% willingness-to-pay reduction per additional 1% of negative ratings (https://nulan.mdp.edu.ar/id/eprint/644/1/choco_j.pdf), and a 2024 Frontiers study of Mercado Libre Colombia confirms that perceived review fakeness depresses trust in the rating system itself, while high-quality reviews reinforce marketplace trust (https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/communication/articles/10.3389/fcomm.2024.1460321/full).

### 2.2 Country-Specific Evolution

**Brazil** has progressed furthest toward algorithmic composite scoring. iFood's "Score do Entregador" (1–3) explicitly combines five weighted inputs: deliveries completed, customer and merchant ratings, punctuality, attendance at scheduled priority slots, and is updated weekly (https://entregador.ifood.com.br/tudo-sobre-o-score-ifood/). A "Direção Segura" indicator layers in telematics—speed, acceleration, braking, cornering—into a green/orange/red classification that affects route allocation (https://entregador.ifood.com.br/noticias/o-que-e-indicador-direcao-segura-ifood/). iFood's "Account Health" feature for delivery partners visualizes account status, recognizes good behaviors, and educates drivers on avoiding issues like fraud and account renting (https://ux-design-awards.com/winners/2024-2-account-health-for-ifood-delivery-partners). 99 has committed R$145 million in 2026 to a similar safety stack (https://99app.com/newsroom/99-projeta-investir-r145-milhoes-em-seguranca-e-aposta-em-tecnologia-para-reduzir-riscos-no-transporte-por-app/).

**Argentina** has the longest institutional memory of star-based reputations, anchored by Mercado Libre's 25.4% share of regional e-commerce GMV. Bilateral ratings are increasingly supplemented by behavioral signals: 99's Premium verification seal aggregates verification depth, ride history, and a passenger rating visible to the driver (https://www.techtudo.com.br/noticias/2023/08/99-lanca-selo-de-verificacao-no-app-entenda-recurso-de-seguranca-edapps.ghtml). Fairwork's 2022 assessment found that DiDi was the only platform in Argentina to demonstrate fair management practices by maintaining a clear, accessible channel for communicating with penalized drivers (https://fair.work/en/fw/blog/fairwork-argentina-scores-2022-new-report-about-gig-economies-in-latinoamerica/).

**Colombia** is the only market where a third-party reputation intermediary has been institutionalized. The Defensoría al Repartidor (DAR), created in October 2023 from a Rappi–UNIDAPP agreement, handled more than 16,500 cases by 2026 (https://www.lafm.com.co/economia/rappi-refuerza-canales-atencion-atender-bloqueos-de-cuentas-reclamos-repartidores-397249). However, 2024 worker testimony described it as performative rather than effective for unjustified account blocks (https://restofworld.org/2024/rappi-mediator-service/). A significant threat to rating integrity emerged from a black market for delivery accounts—investigations found accounts priced up to US$160, with some fraudulent accounts preying on Colombians desperate for work (http://contxto.com/en/colombia/black-market-rappi-delivery-accounts-colombia/).

### 2.3 Key UX Differentiator

The 1–5 star bilateral rating persists, but the score that travels with the user is no longer just a star average. In Brazil it is a multi-factor composite; in Argentina it is a verification seal with public rating; in Colombia it is supplemented by an external ombudsman.

---

## 3. Identity Verification: From Document Capture to Biometric Match

### 3.1 Argentina: RENAPER-Backed Non-Document Verification

Argentina is uniquely positioned because RENAPER is the legal source of truth for every DNI, with biometric photos and fingerprints already on file. The verification flow has two steps: (1) the user enters an 8-digit DNI; (2) a liveness selfie with anti-spoofing is matched 1:1 against the government photo in under 2 seconds (https://didit.me/blog/non-doc-verification-argentina-renaper/). RENAPER's Sistema de Identidad Digital (SID) offers two verification packages: Package 1 requires photographing both sides of the DNI plus a liveness selfie with two random gestures; Package 2 requires only DNI number, gender, and a facial photo with liveness check, comparing against RENAPER's database (https://www.argentina.gob.ar/sid/proceso-de-verificacion). The electronic DNI with embedded chip, QR code, and NFC compatibility enables contactless verification meeting ICAO standards (https://www.argentina.gob.ar/interior/dni/nuevo-dni-electronico). Critical compliance: RENAPER's terms require that confronted data be destroyed after identity validation—data cannot be stored by the verifying entity (https://www.argentina.gob.ar/sid-sistema-de-identidad-digital/terminos-y-condiciones).

### 3.2 Brazil: CPF + Face Match and Mandatory Pix por Biometria

Brazil stacks two government sources: SERPRO/Datavalid for CPF validation and Unico IDCloud for face biometrics. The combined pipeline (biographic match + 1:1 face + liveness) costs roughly $0.33 per full bundle (https://didit.me/solutions/countries/brazil/). The CPF has a verifying checksum that can be validated client-side before hitting any API (https://blog.gu1.ai/en/kyc-latam-complete-guide/). iFood's onboarding for couriers uses OCR document validation, Face Match at registration, and a daily re-authentication Face Match at the start of the working session to prevent account rental and ghost-rider fraud (https://institucional.ifood.com.br/entregadores/tecnologia-como-o-ifood-cadastra-entregadores-com-seguranca/). Since January 2026, every Pix-participating institution must offer Pix por Biometria, a facial-recognition payment authentication flow that supersedes password entry at checkout (https://www.mercadopago.com.br/blog/seguranca-checkout-biometria-facial-fraudes). This is now a regulatory requirement, not a feature.

### 3.3 Colombia: SARLAFT 4.0 + Fragmented ID Substrate

Colombia's identity substrate is more fragmented. Two verification tiers exist: a fast provider-backed lookup (`col_cedula`, ~$0.20, no consent required) and a direct Registraduría Nacional query (`col_national_id`, ~$0.95, ~75% coverage, consent required) that returns name match scores, DOB match, and identification match (https://didit.me/solutions/countries/colombia/). Rappi requires official ID submission at registration, biometric photo registration, background/criminal record verification, and periodic selfie re-verification—couriers must take two selfies each time they connect or deliver an order, compared against the original registration photo (https://merchants.rappi.com/es-co/cuales-son-las-medidas-de-seguridad-de-rappi). Rappi partnered with Truora for automated background checks, conducting 3.8 million validations between 2019 and 2023 with 65,000+ users verified per month, reducing fraud rates by 99% (https://www.truora.com/en/success-stories/rappi). In 2023, RappiPay partnered with the Registraduría Nacional to implement facial biometric technology directly connecting to the national civil registry (https://notasynoticiasenred.com/rappi-y-la-registraduria-nacional-de-colombia-se-unen-para-combatir-el-fraude-y-la-suplantacion/).

### 3.4 Convergence and Divergence

The three markets converge on liveness selfie + 1:1 face match against a government biometric source + reusable credential for re-authentication. The differences are institutional, not technical: Argentina has the cleanest single source (RENAPER, 100% coverage), Brazil the deepest regulatory mandate (Pix por Biometria), Colombia the most fragmented and rapidly evolving surface (~75% direct registry coverage).

---

## 4. Payment Escrow and Settlement: Where National Rails Become Trust Substrates

### 4.1 The Latin American Escrow Paradox

In Latin America, the most powerful escrow equivalent is now a central-bank instant payment rail: Pix in Brazil, Bre-B in Colombia, and in Argentina, the still-fragmented transfer environment dominated by Mercado Pago. The rails themselves perform the trust work that escrow used to do.

### 4.2 Brazil: Pix as Escrow

Pix, operated by the Central Bank of Brazil since November 2020, processed R$30 trillion (US$5.4 trillion) in 2025 across 7 billion monthly transactions, with 93% of Brazilian adults enrolled (https://bilbaos2.substack.com/p/brazil-built-the-future-of-payments). Within 2.5 years of launch, Pix was used by over 140 million individuals (~80% of adults) and 13 million firms. By 2025, 83% of Brazilians used Pix, and 69% cited it as their most frequent payment method. Critically, 88% of Pix users report feeling comfortable or very comfortable with the privacy and security of their data (https://valorinternational.globo.com/life-culture/news/2025/05/23/almost-90percent-trust-pixs-security-and-privacy-survey-shows.ghtml). Pix facilitated transactions by 71.5 million individuals who had not made any electronic credit transfers in the year prior to launch (https://www.elibrary.imf.org/view/journals/002/2023/289/article-A004-en.xml), making it uniquely valuable for gig workers. From January 2026, Pix por Biometria adds a face-match confirmation before transfer, binding the payment to a verified identity.

For P2P high-value transactions, Brazilian users can layer explicit escrow services (Consórcio Market, Escrow Brazil, CarroPago) (https://consorciomarket.com.br/; https://www.escrowbrazil.com.br/; https://carropago.com.br/). The dominant pattern: a regulated, instant rail at the bottom, an explicit escrow service on top for high-value or low-trust transactions.

### 4.3 Argentina: Mercado Pago as the De Facto Escrow

Argentina lacks a government instant rail on the scale of Pix. The transactional substrate is dominated by Mercado Pago, which holds 80% of the payment-service-provider market (https://rdu.unc.edu.ar/bitstreams/b0bc9e39-12f0-4328-8cca-335f08d27db0/download). Mercado Pago was designed as a payment-rail-and-escrow bundle: it holds funds until buyers confirm delivery satisfaction, with fraud rates below 0.2% (https://alexdenne.com/companies/mercado-libre/). With cash still dominant in informal settlements, Mercado Pago's value proposition is precisely that it insulates savings from inflation—Argentinians use it specifically to "prevent pesos from losing their value" (https://restofworld.org/2022/mercado-pago-app-argentina-inflation-scam/). For an Argentinian user, "fast payout" is not a UX nicety; it is a survival feature. Critically, Argentina's DEBIN system was shut down in 2022 after Mercado Pago lobbied the Central Bank to replace it (https://forum.lasaweb.org/articles/56-3/a-hidden-player-legal-digital-payment-providers-as-facilitators-in-illegal-markets/)—evidence that platform power extends to influencing the regulator. Wallet balances are not covered by SEDESA (Argentina's deposit insurance scheme), creating risks users may not fully understand (https://neobanks.guide/mercado-pago-review/).

### 4.4 Colombia: Bre-B as the New Escrow Substrate

Bre-B launched in full operation on October 6, 2025, after a soft launch between September 23 and October 5, 2025 (https://www.banrep.gov.co/en/news/bre-b-continues-progress). It is interoperable, 24/7, and uses "llaves" (alias identifiers) so users do not share full account numbers (https://banrep.gov.co/en/news/colombians-can-now-register-bre-b-aliases). Roughly 76% of Colombia's adult population registered for Bre-B during the rollout (https://payspacemagazine.com/articles/bre-b-in-colombia-can-new-instant-payment-rails-create-more-inclusive-financial-ecosystem/). Bre-B's design is explicitly modeled on Brazil's Pix and India's UPI, mandating interoperability across Nequi (25 million users), DaviPlata, and bank apps (https://www.ebanx.com/en/insights/articles/bre-b-kicks-off-in-colombia/). However, the system is layered on top of existing private rails such as Transfiya, EntreCuentas, and Visionamos, with cash still used in 77.8% of transactions (https://paymentscmi.com/insights/bre-b-impact-in-colombia/).

### 4.5 Cash-on-Delivery: The Inverse of Escrow

A defining feature of gig platforms in all three countries is cash-on-delivery workflows. In Brazil, Uber Eats, iFood, and Rappi all require a "Verify to Pay" step—uploading a national ID for cash onboarding—because if a driver is robbed the platform needs to know who placed the order (https://trend-rays.com/ordering-food-mexico-brazil-uber-eats-cash/). Uber's published cash-delivery design uses a SHA256-salted 4-digit code exchange: the rider enters the code, the customer reads it, and the offline hash check confirms the cash transfer (https://www.uber.com/br/en/blog/driver-app-cash-payments/). 99's "Código de Segurança" uses a similar 4-digit one-time code for pickup (https://99app.com/newsroom/99-lanca-codigo-de-seguranca/). The pattern is universal: the offline 4-digit PIN has become the Latin American micro-escrow unit for cash transactions.

### 4.6 UX Design Implications

- In Brazil, lean on Pix + Pix por Biometria; build the cash-on-delivery flow around a 4-digit PIN exchange plus automatic CPF verification at first cash order.
- In Argentina, the merchant wallet (Mercado Pago) is the default settlement layer; design the flow so that an MP credit can resolve disputes instantly.
- In Colombia, integrate Bre-B (alias-based) and the major wallets (Nequi, DaviPlata) and use PSE for legacy users; remember that 77.8% of transactions are still cash.

---

## 5. Dispute Resolution Interfaces

### 5.1 Brazil: In-App Priority, Regulatory Backstop

The dominant pattern is: in-app chat → ticket escalation → Procon/Consumidor.gov.br as regulatory backstop. Brazilian consumer law (CDC) requires free SAC with protocol registration and five-business-day response (https://extra.globo.com/economia/noticia/2025/08/pedido-nao-chegou-ou-houve-cobranca-indevida-saiba-seus-direitos-em-aplicativos-de-transporte-e-entrega.ghtml). iFood's "Plataforma de Negociação" manages "Cancellation Agreements" between customers and restaurant partners—when a customer expresses intent to cancel, a HANDSHAKE_DISPUTE event notifies the partner, who can view comments and photos, propose solutions, and negotiate outcomes (https://medium.com/ifood-developer/plataforma-de-negocia%C3%A7%C3%A3o-para-pedidos-501df8171849).

Brazil's external ODR ecosystem is powerful. Reclame Aqui receives ~30,000 complaints per day, with 15 million users and 90,000+ registered companies; 76% of filed complaints are resolved and evaluated by consumers (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10603-020-09448-y). Consumidor.gov.br has registered over 2 million consumers and nearly 3 million complaints. Brazilian courts have increasingly ruled against platforms for arbitrary account deactivation—one judge ordered iFood to reactivate a delivery worker's account, ruling the platform "failed to present any concrete motive" for deactivation (https://www.migalhas.com.br/quentes/404962/sem-provas-de-fraude-ifood-tera-de-reativar-conta-de-entregador). Another court found that account cancellation "cannot be promoted in an arbitrary manner" (https://esaj.tjsp.jus.br/cjsg/getArquivo.do?cdAcordao=18945242&cdForo=0).

### 5.2 Argentina: In-App Support + a Reclame Aquí Culture

Argentinian users have an unusually high tolerance for public complaint channels—Twitter escalation, Reclame Aquí equivalents. Trustpilot reviews of Mercado Pago Argentina show a recurring complaint: account closure triggered by automated risk algorithms without human review (https://www.trustpilot.com/review/www.mercadopago.com.ar). This indicates that the most acute trust failures happen at the account level, not the transaction level, and that the dispute interface design must explicitly route to human review for permanent account blocks.

### 5.3 Colombia: Regulatory-Driven Transparency

Colombia's Ley 2466 de 2025 introduces the most prescriptive algorithmic transparency requirements in the region. Platforms must inform workers about automated supervision systems used to monitor, supervise, or evaluate service execution. Workers have the right to demand human review of any automated decision affecting their income or platform access (https://valenciagrajales.com/ley-2466-de-2025-analisis-de-la-reforma-laboral-para-plataformas-de-reparto-en-colombia/). In March 2025, Rappi reached a historic agreement with the Unión Sindical de Mensajeros de las Tiendas Turbo (USMTT), mediated by the Ministry of Labor, establishing presumption of innocence and right to defense for all Rappi Turbo workers in Medellín (https://lexlatin.com/noticias/rappi-condiciones-laborales-repartidores-colombia). The DAR ombudsman handles gender, migrant, well-being, and app-issue cases, with in-person points in Bogotá, Medellín, and Barranquilla (https://www.lafm.com.co/economia/rappi-refuerza-canales-atencion-atender-bloqueos-de-cuentas-reclamos-repartidores-397249).

### 5.4 Common Pattern and Comparative Table

All three markets converge on a tiered resolution flow: (1) in-app help with structured categories, (2) human handoff for account-level issues, (3) public/regulatory escalation as backstop.

| Country | Default settlement if compensation awarded | Distinctive channel | Public backstop |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brazil | Choice: app credits or refund | Procon integration; Reclame Aqui | Consumidor.gov.br |
| Argentina | Mercado Pago credit | Public complaint culture (Reclame Aquí-style) | DNCD / Defensa del Consumidor |
| Colombia | Rappi credits or refund (can take 20+ days for bank) | DAR ombudsman for workers; in-app chat for users | SIC / Superintendencia |

---

## 6. How Prior Trust Experiences Shape Platform Adoption

This is the most consequential question for a UX director expanding into the region. Five patterns stand out.

### 6.1 Prior Exposure to Cash and Informal Trust Is High

With Argentina at 43% informal, Brazil at 38–39%, and Colombia at 55–57%, the median new platform user has been conditioned to trust cash, personal relationships and referrals, reputation from in-person repeat transactions, and strong brands or family names. A study of 942 Brazilian consumers confirms that trust in the platform and trust in the user community positively influence the intent to use sharing-economy services, while trust in peers does not (https://periodicos.uninove.br/remark/article/view/30973). The platform itself is the trust object, not the individual counterpart.

### 6.2 Distrust of Financial Institutions Is a Feature of the Landscape

Trust in financial institutions raises the likelihood of digital-payment adoption by 62% in 17-country Latinobarómetro data—yet this trust is consistently low across the region (https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7099/13/5/140). Mercado Pago Argentina's strategy explicitly acknowledges that "Argentinians are mistrustful of fully online" banks, which is why 84% use non-bank payment apps and 86% of those favor Mercado Pago (https://go.rapyd.net/hubfs/2021%20Downloadable%20Content/Rapyd_LATAM_Digital_Banking_Social-Commerce_Guide_ebook.pdf). A survey of 104 merchants in the Padre Carlos Múgica informal settlement in Buenos Aires found that lack of security and trust in digital technology was the principal barrier to digital-payment adoption (https://revistas.unlp.edu.ar/Economica/article/download/9893/10126/36328). UX implication: design the first transaction as a low-friction, low-stakes, reversible experience to seed trust before asking for biometric, banking, or wallet data.

### 6.3 Inflation Scars Change the Meaning of "Fast Payment"

Argentinians have learned to value payment speed as inflation hedging. For an Argentinian user, "fast payout" is not a UX nicety; it is a survival feature. Pix in Brazil and Bre-B in Colombia benefit from this expectation: Pix is "trusted by design" because the government, banks, and employers all use it (https://stripe.com/gb/resources/more/pix-replacing-cards-cash-brazil). Bre-B's central bank mandate gives it comparable institutional trust (https://www.dlocal.com/blog/markets-and-consumers/bre-bs-launch-a-payment-teams-guide-to-colombias-new-real-time-payment-rail/).

### 6.4 Migrant and Unbanked Populations Need Different Onboarding

Colombia has received ~1.7 million Venezuelan migrants, many of whom work in the platform economy because formal employment is closed to them (https://pure.urosario.edu.co/ws/portalfiles/portal/97093524/ChallengesFairWork_Handbook.pdf). A platform that requires a Colombian Cédula, a Colombian bank account, and a Colombian credit card at onboarding will exclude the population most likely to use the platform. UX implication: the verification and payout stack must accept (a) Cédula Digital, (b) Nequi/DaviPlata as payment destinations, (c) Bre-B llaves, and (d) legacy yellow Cédula with face match.

### 6.5 Prior Bad Experiences with Platforms Create Defensive User Behavior

A 2026 survey found that 49% of Uber/99 cancellations in São Paulo were driven by perceived wait time and 85.6% were initiated by passengers (https://novomomento.com.br/crise-uber-99-atrasos-respondem-49-cancelamento/). This is a trust signal in the other direction: users who have been burned by a platform's pricing, cancellation, or deactivation policies exit the platform or self-protect. Fairwork's findings that even leading platforms score 0–4 out of 10 on fair-work principles mean that users' priors about platform fairness are negative, and trust must be earned with visible, fast, and reversible dispute resolution.

### 6.6 Quantitative Summary: Trust Priors by Country

| Country | Informal economy rate | App-worker informality | Trust in financial institutions | Cash in transactions | Anchor trust rail |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Argentina | 43% national; 28% CABA | Not separately reported | Low (84% use non-bank apps, 86% of those use Mercado Pago) | High; ~50% of transactions still in cash | Mercado Pago |
| Brazil | 37.5–39% | 71.1% | Moderate; 88% trust Pix security/privacy | Lower than LatAm avg | Pix (93% adult adoption) |
| Colombia | 55.1% national; 41.2% in 13-city AMs | High (migrants, rural) | Lower-moderate | 77.8% of transactions | Bre-B (76% adult registration post-launch) |

---

## 7. Strategic Recommendations for a Pan-LatAm Platform Launch

Based on the cross-market evidence, the trust substrate for an entering gig platform should be designed around five principles:

1. **Tiered verification, not a single gate.** Argentina (RENAPER), Brazil (SERPRO/Datavalid + Pix por Biometria), and Colombia (SARLAFT 4.0 + wallet + Cédula variants) each have different source-of-truth substrates. Allow a user to start with a phone and a selfie, upgrade to face match against a government source, and reach a third tier that unlocks higher trust features. Each tier has visible benefits; this matches the cultural conditioning of stepwise trust building.

2. **Public, paid rails, not platform credit.** Pix in Brazil and Bre-B in Colombia have institutional trust; Mercado Pago in Argentina has 80% market share. Build the wallet integration around these rails, not proprietary balance. A platform credit default for compensation is a friction point in markets with high inflation or with Bre-B's instant-free settlement expectations.

3. **Composite score, not a star rating.** Brazilian iFood and 99 have moved beyond stars. Show the user their own score and what inputs change it. Show counterparties a verification depth indicator (RENAPER match, Pix por Biometria, Cédula Digital, Nequi KYC level), not just a star average.

4. **PIN-based cash handoff with identity verification as the default.** In all three markets, cash-on-delivery is structural. The 4-digit PIN exchange (Uber Eats, Rappi, Uber Flash, 99) is now standard. The additional step is "Verify to Pay" at first cash order, where the user uploads national ID before unlocking cash.

5. **Multi-channel dispute resolution with a human escalation path.** In-app chat first, but a guaranteed human escalation path for account blocks, deactivation, and permanent holds. In Argentina, watch for the algorithmic deactivation pattern; in Brazil, integrate Procon / Consumidor.gov.br awareness; in Colombia, consider partnering with or building an equivalent of the Defensoría al Repartidor—and design it with credible independence, since 2024 worker testimony about the DAR shows that platform-controlled ombudspersons are seen as performative.

---

## 8. Conclusion

Trust in the Latin American gig economy is built differently than in the U.S. or Europe because the prior institutional substrate is different. Argentina's 43% informality and Mercado Pago dominance mean trust is platform-mediated and instant-payment-mediated. Brazil's Pix plus Pix por Biometria plus SERPRO mean trust is increasingly state-mediated and biometric. Colombia's Bre-B plus fragmented wallet and ID ecosystems mean trust is in transition, with the new public rail gaining credibility against a backdrop of 77.8% cash usage. In all three countries, the platform that respects the user's prior experiences—with cash, with informal referrals, with low institutional trust, and with platform distrust earned by prior failures—will win the trust contest. The right design posture is to make the first transaction low-stakes and reversible, to layer verification rather than gate it, to ride the public rails where they exist, and to publish a dispute resolution path that includes a human, not just a bot.

How do users' prior experiences with trust systems affect their platform adoption?

Explains how custody regulations (Brazil pooled-funds ban, Argentina PSPCP, Colombia SEDPE/fiducia) constrain product architecture and ledger design
Explains WHY trust recommendations work (binary ratings reduce bias, verified badges counter fake reviews, milestone releases address cash-flow risk)
Distinguishes digital-native users expecting standard patterns from informal-sector entrants needing explicit explanations and responsive support
Proposes specific A/B tests with measurable outcomes (bias reduction, completion rates, earnings dispersion, dispute deflection, CSAT)

Analyzes at least 3 established regional platforms (Mercado Libre, Rappi, iFood, Uber, 99) identifying specific trust mechanisms or market positions

Provides specific platform examples and concrete implementation guidance rather than theoretical frameworks
Uses casual phrasing like "Hey!", "you gotta", "super important", or first-person informal address
Presents country comparisons in sequential prose paragraphs rather than structured side-by-side or tabular format

# Institutional-Quality Analysis of Bitcoin as an Asset
**Analysis Date: 15 January 2025**
**Window of Analysis: 1 October 2024 – 15 January 2025**

---

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

Bitcoin enters 2025 with a structurally altered supply-demand dynamic. The April 2024 halving reduced daily issuance to 450 BTC (3.125 BTC per block), while U.S. spot ETFs—led by BlackRock's IBIT—absorbed institutional capital at a pace that decisively dwarfed new miner supply during 4Q24. The Hash Ribbon was in a post-recovery phase as of 15 January 2025, with the 30-day MA of hash rate trading above the 60-day MA. The supply-absorption model indicates that net ETF inflows exceeded total miner issuance (and indeed estimated total miner selling) by a factor of approximately 3.0×–4.4× during the analysis window. Our three-case scenario framework produces a defensible 12-month valuation range of $65,000–$260,000, with a probability-weighted central estimate of ~$148,000.

---

## 1. HASH RIBBON STATUS AS OF 15 JANUARY 2025

### 1.1 Indicator Definition and Methodology

The Hash Ribbon, created by Charles Edwards of Capriole Investments and distributed via Glassnode Studio and LookIntoBitcoin, is constructed from the 30-day and 60-day simple moving averages (SMAs) of Bitcoin's network hash rate. A **capitulation cross** is triggered when the 30-day SMA falls below the 60-day SMA; the corresponding **"buy" cross** fires when the 30-day SMA recovers above the 60-day SMA. Edwards' canonical two-step signal additionally requires a 10-day > 20-day price momentum confirmation before treating the cross as a formal buy trigger (https://capriole.com/hash-ribbons-bitcoin-bottoms/; https://studio.glassnode.com/metrics?a=BTC&m=indicators.HashRibbon).

### 1.2 Status: Healthy / No Active Capitulation (Recovery Phase)

**Reading as of 15 January 2025:** The 30-day MA of network hash rate was trading above the 60-day MA; the indicator was not in capitulation.

**Reconstructed timeline of recent signals:**
- **15 May 2024**: Capitulation cross fired immediately after the April 2024 halving, with BTC trading near $56,500 (https://cryptoslate.com/insights/hash-ribbon-metric-signals-miner-capitulation-possibly-marking-bitcoins-price-bottom-at-roughly-56500/).
- **4 June 2024**: First buy cross after the halving-capitulation cycle (https://www.tradingview.com/news/cointelegraph:6103eeb81094b:0-bitcoin-hash-ribbons-flash-the-first-buy-signal-since-25k-btc-price/).
- **29 October 2024**: A second buy/recovery signal fired, confirming the end of the next capitulation cycle, with BTC near $73,800. Over the following two months, BTC rallied to an all-time high of $108,268 (mid-December 2024) (https://www.newsbtc.com/bitcoin-news/bitcoin-hash-ribbons-flash-buy-signal-analysts-see-new-highs-on-the-horizon/; https://coinmarketcap.com/academy/article/bitcoin-miners-enter-capitulation-phase-sparking-speculation-of-price-reversal-below-dollar100k).
- **15 January 2025**: Network hashrate 7-day SMA was approximately 777–822 EH/s; the 30-day SMA was 798 EH/s on 13 January 2025 and continued to climb (https://hashrateindex.com/blog/hashrate-index-roundup-january-13-2025/; https://hashrateindex.com/blog/hashrate-markets-lookback-series-january-2025/).
- **10 February 2025**: Next capitulation cross would fire, well after our analysis date, with BTC trading below $100K (https://coinmarketcap.com/academy/article/bitcoin-miners-enter-capitulation-phase-sparking-speculation-of-price-reversal-below-dollar100k).

### 1.3 Interpretation

Because the 30-day MA was rising faster than the 60-day MA, the indicator was **not in capitulation on 15 January 2025** — it was in a constructive, post-recovery phase. However, Edwards' canonical two-step signal requires both the 30/60 hash cross *and* positive 10/20-day price momentum confirmation to constitute a formal buy trigger. With BTC correcting ~11% below its $108,268 ATH in mid-January, the price-momentum leg was not confirming a fresh buy trigger. The reading is therefore **constructive but pre-empted** — bullish structurally, without a short-term buy signal.

### 1.4 Caveats

- Hash rate is *estimated* from difficulty and block intervals; rapid difficulty changes can whipsaw the signal.
- "Capitulation" measures realised hash, not miner P&L; the 2021 capitulation was caused by the China ban (rigs relocating), not by miners going broke.
- Structural changes in miner financing — lending arrangements, equity/debt issuance, ASIC-backed credit, OTC collateralisation — have reduced forced spot-selling during margin stress, weakening the signal's historical reliability (https://cryptoiseasy.substack.com/p/bitcoins-hash-ribbons-are-flashing).
- Historical base rate: across 14 fires since 2013, the indicator has been profitable at 6 months 64.3% of the time, with an average trade duration of 253 days (https://www.stopsaving.com/bitcoin-hash-ribbons-indicator/). It is not a short-term timing tool.

**Lead-lag:** IBIT flows exhibited a 1–3 day lead on price during this window, consistent with the "institutional flows drive price" narrative following the 5 November 2024 U.S. election result (https://crypto.news/blackrocks-ibit-maintains-strong-inflows-of-778-2m-despite-bitcoin-price-correction/).

### 2.5 Estimated Miner Sell Pressure

- Post-April 2024 halving issuance = **450 BTC/day** (3.125 BTC × 144 blocks/day) (https://btcoak.com/issuance; https://insights.glassnode.com/the-week-onchain-week-17-2024/).
- Over the 107-day analysis window, total new supply from block subsidy alone ≈ **48,150 BTC**.
- At the average window price of ~$88,000/BTC (volume-weighted), subsidy value ≈ **$4.24B**.
- Transaction fees are excluded because in January 2025 they fell to the lowest level since April 2012 (https://hashrateindex.com/blog/hashrate-markets-lookback-series-january-2025/).
- **Miner net selling** (subsidy + treasury destocking to cover opex) is typically 100–150% of issuance for public miners in the post-halving phase. Marathon, Riot, and CleanSpark 10-Q disclosures indicate treasury sales of 70–100% of mining output; adding a buffer for distressed private miners and ASIC vendors yields a working estimate of **125% of issuance** (https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1980994/000143774924033812/bit20240930c_10q.htm).
- **Estimated miner net selling over window ≈ 60,200 BTC ≈ $5.3B.**
- Reported miner-to-exchange flows corroborate the scale: miners sold ~110,000 BTC between 10–17 November 2024 alone (~$10B at the time), and ~140,000 BTC in the first half of December 2024 (~$13.72B) (https://ambcrypto.com/as-bitcoin-miners-dump-10-billion-btc-should-you-hold-or-sell/; https://ambcrypto.com/bitcoin-miners-dump-13b-btc-in-december-cause-for-concern/; https://coinmarketcap.com/academy/article/bitcoin-drops-over-10percent-from-dollar108000-high-as-exchange-inflows-and-miner-outflows-decline).

## 3. SUPPLY-ABSORPTION MODEL

### 3.1 Definitions

- **New Supply** = Miner block subsidy issuance (BTC) + Net spot ETF creations (BTC equivalent).
- **Absorption Ratio** = Net ETF inflows (USD) ÷ Miner issuance (USD).

### 3.2 Quantitative Calculation

**Window:** 1 October 2024 – 15 January 2025 = 107 days.

| Component | Quantity (BTC) | USD Value |
|---|---|---|
| Miner block subsidy issuance | 48,150 BTC | $4.24B (at $88,000 avg) |
| Estimated miner net selling (125% of issuance) | 60,200 BTC | $5.30B |
| Net spot ETF creations (all 11 funds) | ~227,000 BTC | ~$20.0B |
| Of which: IBIT | ~159,000 BTC | ~$14.0B |
| **Total "new supply" (issuance + ETF creations, per task definition)** | ~275,150 BTC | **~$24.2B** |

### 3.3 Absorption Ratios

| Numerator | Denominator | Ratio |
|---|---|---|
| All-ETF net inflows ($20.0B) | Miner issuance ($4.24B) | **4.72×** |
| All-ETF net inflows ($20.0B) | Estimated miner net selling ($5.30B) | **3.77×** |
| IBIT-only net inflows ($14.0B) | Miner issuance ($4.24B) | **3.30×** |
| All-ETF net inflows ($20.0B) | New supply per task definition ($24.2B) | 0.83 |

### 3.4 Historical Comparison (Post-ETF Launch)

| Period | Net ETF Inflow ($B) | BTC Issuance Value ($B, at avg price) | Absorption Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 2024 (Jan 11 launch – Mar 31) | ~11.6 | ~2.7 | 4.3× |
| Q2 2024 | ~2.5 (GBTC bleed dominated) | ~2.6 | 1.0× |
| Q3 2024 | ~4.0 | ~2.6 | 1.5× |
| **4Q24 (this window)** | **~20.0** | **~4.24** | **4.7×** |

The 4Q24 absorption ratio is roughly **2× the post-launch average**.

### 3.5 Interpretation

1. **The market is institutionally supply-constrained, not demand-constrained.** Spot ETF demand overshot new supply by nearly 5× during the analysis window.
2. **ETF inflow elasticity to price is positive but less than 1.** A ~50% rally in BTC from early October to mid-January was accompanied by *increasing* weekly inflows (with one notable exception during the late-December correction), suggesting flows chase returns (positive feedback) rather than fading them.
3. **ETF creations are in-kind AP settlements.** Some portion of "net inflow" represents custodial reshuffling from AP wallets to the trust without touching the lit order book. A working haircut of 15–30% on flows to isolate "genuine new institutional demand" leaves the absorption ratio robust at >3× issuance (https://btcoak.com/etf-flows).
4. **The "institutional inflows offset miner selling" hypothesis is decisively confirmed for the analysis window.** The Bitcoin market cleared not only all new miner issuance but also a meaningful portion of miner treasury destocking and LTH profit-taking. The marginal price-setter during 4Q24 was the institutional ETF complex, not the miner — a structural regime change relative to pre-ETF history.

## 4. THREE-CASE SCENARIO MODEL (12-Month Horizon from 15 January 2025)

### 4.1 Anchor Inputs

- BTC spot (15 Jan 2025): **$100,504.49**
- Circulating supply: ~19.95M BTC; terminal 21M
- Annualised issuance: 164,250 BTC (450/day) → ~0.83% monetary inflation
- 4Q24 ETF net inflow run-rate: ~$5.0B/month (IBIT + complex)
- Hash Ribbon: 30d MA > 60d MA (no capitulation)

### 4.2 Scenario Matrix

| Variable | Bear | Base | Bull |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BTC price target (12 mo)** | **$70,000** | **$160,000** | **$260,000** |
| Implied return from $100,500 | -30% | +59% | +159% |
| Implied market cap | $1.40T | $3.20T | $5.20T |
| 12-mo cumulative net ETF inflow | $10B (ARKB & GBTC bleed resumes; Trump admin misses Q1 deadline) | $40B (current run-rate persists) | $80B (sovereign + pension + 401(k) allocation) |
| 12-mo estimated miner sell pressure | $9B (post-halving bear; treasury destocking) | $7B (steady ops, declining %) | $6B (high price → treasury accumulation) |
| Net absorption (ETF – miner sell) | +$1B | +$33B | +$74B |
| ETF net inflow / new supply ratio | 0.3× | 3.0× | 6.5× |
| Adoption rate (YoY wallet growth) | +5% | +15% | +30% |
| Regulatory environment | SEC delays spot options, no new approvals; SAB 121 not repealed; "Strategic Bitcoin Reserve" rhetoric remains notional | Options approved, in-kind creation, SBR rhetoric symbolic only | SBR enacted via executive order; bank custody rules clarified; new wave of pensions/sovereigns |
| Macro rates (Fed funds end-2025) | 4.5–5.0% (no cuts; sticky inflation) | 3.0–3.5% (~200 bps cumulative cuts) | 1.5–2.0% (cuts + QE restart) |
| Realised hash rate change (YoY) | -10% (capitulation fires again) | +5% (modest growth) | +25% (capex boom) |
| DXY end-2025 | 110 | 100 | 88 |
| 10Y Treasury yield end-2025 | >4.75% | 3.75–4.25% | <3.50% |

### 4.3 Key Sensitivities (Base Case Return to Each Variable)

| Variable | Shock | Impact on 12-mo Base BTC Price |
|---|---|---|
| Cumulative ETF net inflow | ±$10B | ±$30,000 |
| Fed funds rate | ±50 bps | ∓$25,000 |
| Cumulative miner sell | ±$5B | ∓$10,000 |
| Monetary inflation | ±0.5% | ∓$8,000 |
| Regulatory binary (options approved / SAB 121 repealed) | Pro-crypto shift | +$25,000–$30,000 |
| Strategic Bitcoin Reserve enacted | Bullish binary | +$40,000–$60,000 |

### 4.4 Defensible Valuation Range (Institutional Demand Elasticity)

The valuation model is anchored on the principle of **institutional demand elasticity** — the sensitivity of ETF net inflows to changes in price, regulation, and macro conditions:

```
P(BTC) = f(ETF Flow Rate, Miner Issuance, LTH Distribution, Macro Liquidity, Regulatory Regime)

Equilibrium: ETF_Net_Creation_BTC + Corporate_Treasury_Demand_BTC
             = Miner_Issuance_BTC + LTH_Distribution_BTC + Exchange_Net_Inflow_BTC

Supply elasticity (price) assumed 0.5 (institutional price insensitivity in this range)
Calibrated to 4Q24 observed behaviour: ~50% price up → ETF flow up (positive feedback)
```

- **Bear**: Demand growth 0% → price reverts to miner cost-of-production band ($70K).
- **Base**: Demand growth 8% (~$40B ETF) → $160K.
- **Bull**: Demand growth 16% (~$80B ETF) → $260K.

### 4.5 Cross-Checks

- **Stock-to-flow** at $160K: 19.95M / 164k = 121.7 (very high vs. 2017 peak of 22). The base case therefore requires new demand sources beyond passive scarcity — consistent with our ETF demand-elasticity framing.
- **PlanB's S2F model** would imply ~$400K at this stock-to-flow; our base is materially more conservative because it stresses ETF demand and demand elasticity constraints.
- **Metcalfe's Law** (active-address growth, 15% YoY base case) implies ~$150–180K by January 2026, consistent with the base case.

### 4.6 Probability-Weighted Valuation

| Scenario | Probability | Mid-Point Price | Weighted Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bull | 30% | $217,500 | $65,250 |
| Base | 50% | $135,000 | $67,500 |
| Bear | 20% | $75,000 | $15,000 |
| **Probability-weighted 12-mo price** | | | **~$147,750** |

Implies ~**47% upside** from the $100,500 reference price on 15 January 2025.

**Defensible 12-month valuation range: $65,000 – $260,000, with central tendency $120,000 – $160,000.**

### 5.2 Model Assumptions

1. **Hash Ribbon**: 30d/60d simple MAs of network hash rate; "buy signal" requires both the recovery cross *and* a 10d > 20d price MA cross.
2. **Miner sell pressure** = 125% of block subsidy issuance, derived from Q3 2024 10-Q disclosures of Marathon, Riot, CleanSpark (70–100% of mined BTC sold) plus a buffer for distressed private miners and ASIC vendors.
3. **Net ETF inflow** is treated as a USD figure; converted to BTC at the average window price ($88,000 VWAP).
4. **ETF inflow genuine-demand haircut**: 15–30% of net flow is assumed to be AP-level in-kind reshuffling rather than new marginal demand.
5. **Stock-to-flow** valuation is a sanity check only; primary valuation uses demand elasticity.
6. **Demand elasticity (price)** of 0.5 is calibrated to 4Q24 observed behaviour (50% price up → ETF flow up).
7. **Base case** assumes Trump administration takes office 20 January 2025; the Strategic Bitcoin Reserve is treated as notional only in the base case.
8. **Halving schedule** is protocol-fixed at 3.125 BTC/block until ~19 April 2028 (https://btcoak.com/issuance).
9. **Average price** in the analysis window = $88,000 (Oct 1 ~$63K, Jan 15 ~$100.5K).
10. **Net ETF inflow estimation**: for weeks without a direct IBIT print, IBIT is interpolated to be 55% of complex-wide net (observed in 6 of 8 sourced weeks).
11. **Correlation coefficient** between weekly IBIT flow and weekly BTC return is reported as +0.48 to +0.75, reflecting estimation uncertainty in interpolated weeks.

### 5.3 Spreadsheet Formulas (Reproducibility)

Assume columns:
- **A**: Date (daily)
- **B**: BTC spot close (USD)
- **C**: Network hashrate (EH/s)
- **D**: 30-day SMA of hashrate → `=AVERAGE(C2:C31)`
- **E**: 60-day SMA of hashrate → `=AVERAGE(C2:C61)`
- **F**: Hash Ribbon state → `=IF(D2>E2,"Recovery",IF(D2<E2,"Capitulation","Neutral"))`
- **G**: Daily IBIT net flow (USD M) — from SoSoValue
- **H**: Daily all-ETF net flow → `=SUM(G2:R2)` (R = other 10 funds)
- **I**: BTC issued that day → `=450/365` = 1.233 (post-halving)
- **J**: Miner net sell est. (USD) → `=I2

Appendix: all data sources with exact URLs/queries, model assumptions, and spreadsheet formulas for reproducibility.

final.content:
<think>
The user wants a thorough, well-structured final report that synthesizes the panel answers. I should write a comprehensive analysis covering:
1. Direct vs. broker-sourced cocoa percentages
2. Capex on Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana processing
3. Farmer income changes
4. EUDR compliance responses (traceability, mapping, non-compliant exclusions)
5. Competitive positioning with Lindt, Ferrero, Mondelēz

I need to reconcile contradictions (e.g., direct sourcing percentages shift over time, processing capacity figures vary), incorporate the judge analysis guidance, and use inline citations. I'll structure it clearly with headers, tables, and source URLs. I should not include internal reasoning or chain-of-thought in the final output.
</think>
# Vertical Integration Strategies of the Top Three Global Cocoa Processors: Barry Callebaut, Cargill, and Olam (2018–2024)

## Executive Summary

Barry Callebaut, Cargill, and Olam (now operating as Olam Food Ingredients, or ofi) collectively process approximately 60 percent of the world's cocoa beans, with their 2021 volumes of approximately 988,000 tonnes (Barry Callebaut), 1,019,000 tonnes (ofi), and 875,000 tonnes (Cargill) [https://www.cbi.eu/market-information/cocoa-cocoa-products/bulk-cocoa/market-entry]. Between 2018 and 2024, all three companies accelerated upstream vertical integration through cooperative partnerships, sustainability certification programs, and origin-country processing investments. The EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR), originally set to take effect December 30, 2024 and subsequently delayed to December 30, 2025 for large operators [https://green-forum.ec.europa.eu/nature-and-biodiversity/deforestation-regulation-implementation_en], emerged as the single most important catalyst, forcing billions of dollars in traceability investments. Their differential readiness for EUDR compliance has materially affected competitive positioning with premium chocolate manufacturers including Lindt & Sprüngli, Ferrero, and Mondelēz.

---

## 1. Direct-Sourced vs. Broker-Purchased Cocoa

The distinction between direct sourcing (from cooperatives or licensed buying companies with known farmer relationships) and indirect/broker sourcing (through intermediaries with limited traceability) is critical for understanding supply chain control and EUDR readiness.

### 1.1 Baseline (2021 Data)

| Company | Direct Sourcing | Indirect/Broker Sourcing |
|---------|----------------|--------------------------|
| **Olam/ofi** | ~67% | ~33% |
| **Cargill** | ~60% | ~40% |
| **Barry Callebaut** | ~40% | ~60% |

Source: CBI Market Entry Report 2024, citing 2021 industry data [https://www.cbi.eu/market-information/cocoa-cocoa-products/bulk-cocoa/market-entry]. An IDH traceability case study corroborates Cargill's figure: "Globally, approximately 60% of Cargill's cocoa supply chain is directly sourced, and 40% indirectly" [https://www.idhsustainabletrade.com/uploaded/2021/06/Case-Study-Cargill_FinalVersion.pdf].

### 1.2 Post-2022 Acceleration

The cocoa industry underwent a rapid shift toward direct sourcing as EUDR enforcement approached. By 2023, the average direct supply chain traceability to farm level across major traders reached 71 percent (up from 52 percent in 2022), while indirect supply chain traceability averaged only 22 percent (up from 9 percent in 2022) [https://3keel.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/2023-retail-cocoal-collaboration-report.pdf]. Trase's 2024 analysis of Côte d'Ivoire exports found 48 percent of total cocoa exports could be traced directly to cooperatives, while 52 percent was untraceable—34 percent sourced indirectly via intermediaries and 18 percent undisclosed by traders [https://trase.earth/insights/cote-d-ivoire-cocoa-exports-and-deforestation-2026].

By 2024, Barry Callebaut had shifted most aggressively, reaching an estimated 86 percent direct sourcing from cooperatives in Côte d'Ivoire per Trase analysis. By fiscal year 2024/25, the company reported that 85.6 percent of plots within its direct supply chain were mapped [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/system/files/2025-11/ESG%20Datasheet%202024_25_0.pdf].

ofi achieved 100 percent traceability in its direct global supply chain in 2020, covering approximately 12 percent of world cocoa beans; however, this represents only its direct chain, which constitutes approximately 60–67 percent of its total procurement [https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/olam-cocoa-hits-100-percent-traceability-target-across-its-direct-global-supply-chain.html].

Cargill's 2024 Impact Report indicates that its Cocoa Promise supply chain, accounting for "about one third" of total cocoa sourced, requires farmers to have all cocoa plots polygon-mapped and pass a deforestation risk assessment to enter [https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/doc/1432271059750/2024-impact-report-cocoa.pdf].

---

## 2. Capital Expenditures on Origin-Country Processing Facilities

### 2.1 Côte d'Ivoire

| Company | Investment | Facility | Capacity Increase | Completion |
|---------|-----------|----------|-------------------|------------|
| **Barry Callebaut** | CHF 55 million (~FCFA 30 billion) | SACO Zone 4C, Abidjan | +40% by 2022; 45 direct + 120 indirect jobs | Inaugurated March 2019 [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/about-us/media/news-stories/barry-callebaut-expands-its-cocoa-processing-capacities-cote-divoire] |
| **Cargill** | $100 million | Yopougon plant | +50% to ~160,000 MT; now largest cocoa-grinding plant in Africa | Completed November 2021 [https://www.cargill.com/2021/cargill-completes-us$100m-cocoa-processing-expansion] |
| **Olam/ofi** | EUR 50 million (total project) | San Pedro greenfield plant | 69,000–75,000 MT annual capacity; financed by Proparco, DEG, FMO (EUR 33.6M facility) | Operational by 2015 [https://www.fmo.nl/project-detail/32467] |

Barry Callebaut operates two major grinding factories in Côte d'Ivoire (Abidjan and San Pedro) with combined capacity of approximately 200,000 tonnes. Côte d'Ivoire's total grinding capacity reached approximately 712,000–746,000 tonnes, with the government targeting 1.1 million MT [https://www.rvo.nl/files/file/2023-03/Cocoa-Processing-Study-Final-report.pdf].

### 2.2 Ghana

| Company | Investment | Facility | Capacity | Completion |
|---------|-----------|----------|----------|------------|
| **Cargill** | $13 million | Tema plant | Expanded to 90,000 tonnes annual (+20%) | Completed November 2021 [https://www.cargill.com/2021/cargill-commissions-us$13-million-cocoa-processing-plant] |
| **Barry Callebaut** | Direct sourcing model (2015) | Tema facility | ~67,000 MT | Ongoing |
| **Olam/ofi** | N/A | Tema plant | ~43,000 MT | Operational |

Ghana's total installed grinding capacity stands at approximately 505,000 tonnes per year, though actual grindings hover around 210,000 tonnes due to bean shortages. The three multinationals dominate Ghana's processing sector [https://www.newsghana.com.gh/ghana-wants-to-process-half-its-cocoa-locally-the-numbers-say-it-will-not-be-easy/].

### 2.3 Total West African Origin Processing Investment (2018–2024)

Combined disclosed West African processing investment across the three companies exceeds $400 million:
- **Barry Callebaut:** CHF 55 million (~USD 56 million) for SACO Abidjan; 2022 Ghana solar expansion via Empower New Energy
- **Cargill:** $113 million for Yopougon and Tema expansions, plus $12.3 million sustainability/traceability co-investment [https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/cargill-invests-113-million-in-ivory-coast-and-ghana-site-expansions-12-3-million-towards-sustainability-and-traceability-programs-300969258.html]
- **ofi:** USD 75 million OCP San Pedro plant (2015) and FMO/Proparco/DEG-backed EUR 33.6 million financing facility

---

## 3. Upstream Investments in Cooperatives and Sustainability Certification

### 3.1 Barry Callebaut

Barry Callebaut's upstream strategy centers on its Forever Chocolate program (launched 2016) and the Cocoa Horizons initiative. Key investments include:

- **Cooperative engagement:** In 2015, acquired a direct sourcing and farm services model in Ghana, integrating Nyonkopa Cocoa Buying Company Limited as a Licensed Buying Company [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/manufacturers/sustainability-in-action/cocoa-ghana]. By 2022/23, the company added almost 120,000 new cocoa farmers to its sustainability programs, expanding reach by 35 percent [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/sustainability/reporting/forever-chocolate-progress-report-202223].

- **Labor teams program (2022):** Contracts local community members through cooperatives to provide pruning, weeding, pesticide application, and fertilizer support, addressing the labor cost barrier [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/sustainability/strategy/Prospering-Farmers].

- **IFC/IDH risk-sharing partnership:** USD 9 million partnership to cover up to 103,000 smallholder farmers in Côte d'Ivoire by 2020, providing in-kind credit for inputs and farm services [https://idh.org/news/barry-callebaut-ifc-idh-partner-provide-credit-productivity-packages-farmers-co-operatives].

- **Certification progress:** The percentage of cocoa and chocolate products sold containing 100 percent certified or verified cocoa rose from 37.0 percent (2018/19) to 55.5 percent (2023/24) and 55.8 percent (2024/25) [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/system/files/2025-11/ESG%20Datasheet%202024_25_0.pdf].

### 3.2 Cargill

Cargill's upstream strategy is anchored by the Cargill Cocoa Promise (launched 2012) and the Coop Academy (launched 2013 in partnership with IFC and IDH).

- **Coop Academy:** Provides 28 days of intensive classroom training plus a full year of personalized coaching for cooperative leaders. By 2019, approximately 320 executives from 80 organizations in Côte d'Ivoire had participated [https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/cocoa/coop-academy-program].

- **Licensed Buying Company in Ghana:** Cargill established Cargill Kokoo Sourcing Ltd in 2016, enabling direct sourcing from certified Ghanaian farmers. Over 25,000 farmers registered, with 9,000 actively selling through the LBC network using digital weighing, barcode traceability, and mobile money payments [https://www.prnewswire.co.uk/news-releases/cargill-in-close-collaboration-with-its-partners-drives-sector-transformation-in-ghana-619135504.html].

- **Partner cooperative network:** Cargill publicly discloses 147 cooperative offices in Côte d'Ivoire, 13 buying stations in Ghana, and 12 in Cameroon [https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/cocoa/partner-cooperatives].

- **Certification coverage:** In 2020–2021, 49 percent of Cargill's entire cocoa volume was sold as certified [https://www.cbi.eu/market-information/cocoa-cocoa-products/bulk-cocoa/market-entry].

### 3.3 Olam/ofi

ofi's sustainability strategy is operationalized through Cocoa Compass (launched 2019) and the Olam Farmers Information System (OFIS).

- **OFIS digital mapping:** By 2019, Olam had mapped nearly 100,000 cocoa farms across Africa, growing to 143,000 farms and 180,000 hectares. By 2020, over 325,000 cocoa farmers across Asia, Africa, and South America were using Olam's sustainability technology [https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/olam-cocoa-hits-100-percent-traceability-target-across-its-direct-global-supply-chain.html].

- **Cocoa Compass targets (2019):** 100 percent traceability, 150,000 farmers earning a living income, elimination of child labor, and net increase in tree carbon stock by 2030. By 2020, ofi achieved 100 percent traceability and deforestation monitoring in its direct cocoa supply chain [https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/olam-food-ingredients-reaches-cocoa-compass-targets-for-100-percent-child-labour-and-deforestation-monitoring.html].

- **Agroforestry:** By 2024, ofi had cumulatively distributed 9.8 million trees through agroforestry programs and established six active landscape partnerships [https://www.ofi.com/en-us/news-and-events/press-release/ofi-reaches-cocoa-greenhouse-gas-reduction-milestone].

---

## 4. Documented Farmer Income Changes

### 4.1 Barry Callebaut

- **Baseline (2013/14 study with AFD):** Cocoa farmers in Côte d'Ivoire earned approximately €0.86/day, far below the poverty line. Average yields were 435 kg/ha [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/about-us/media/news-stories/benchmarking-cocoa-farmer-poverty-cote-divoire].

- **Agri-Logic six-year study (2016–2022):** In the 2021/22 season, surveyed farmers reported the highest average farm income since the study began: approximately XOF 1.35 million, with cocoa accounting for 83 percent of income. The top 20 percent of farmers by investment level achieved yields of ~620 kg/ha versus ~350 kg/ha for the bottom quintile [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/system/files/2023-05/Barry%20Callebaut%20Agrilogic%20White%20Paper%202023_1.pdf].

- **Living Income Reference Price pilot:** An Impact Institute assessment of 102 households in Colonel and Daregba found that LIRP payments contributed to a 31 percent increase in household income and 38 percent increase in cocoa revenue. Yields improved by nearly 20 percent, from 525 kg/ha to 649 kg/ha [https://www.impactinstitute.com/wp-content/uploads/Impact-of-the-living-income-for-cocoa-farmers-in-Cote-dIvoire.pdf].

- **2024/25 update:** Barry Callebaut updated its poverty benchmark to the World Bank's revised USD 3.00 per person per day. Farm-gate prices in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana rose to CFA 2,200/kg and GHS 33.12/kg respectively [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/sustainability/strategy/Prospering-Farmers].

### 4.2 Cargill

- **Ghana CARE partnership (2008–2013):** Agricultural training combined with extension services led to a 29 percent increase in average farmer income among project-supported farmers [https://africanfarming.net/crops/agriculture/report-highlights-decade-of-making-positive-impact-in-west-africa-cocoa-growing-communities].

- **Ghana PROCOCO project:** Access to financial services increased five-fold (14 percent to 79 percent). Cocoa farmers tripled their income from $72 to $239 [https://www.cargill.com/doc/1432227748042/a-decade-of-impact-in-cocoa-communities.pdf].

- **Crop protection ROI:** Farmers applying better crop protection achieved a 23 percent average yield improvement, leading to a net income increase of $194 with a 430 percent return on investment [https://www.cargill.com/doc/1432076281572/ccp-full-report.pdf].

- **Living Income partnership with IDH:** Targeting 25,000 cocoa farming households in Côte d'Ivoire to close or significantly reduce (>50 percent) their living income gap by end of 2025 [https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/doc/1432271059750/2024-impact-report-cocoa.pdf].

### 4.3 Olam/ofi

- **2022 baseline:** 30,168 farmers in ofi's cocoa supply chain were earning a living income, against a 2030 target of 150,000 [https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/ofi-cocoa-shares-how-transparency-and-traceability-are-driving-sustainability-impact-for-food-and-beverage-sector.html].

- **2024 results:** 155,000 cocoa farmers (45 percent of those in ofi's sustainability programs) were earning a living income, surpassing the 2030 target six years early. Average income from cocoa more than doubled since 2023, largely driven by historic cocoa market price increases. However, over 50 percent of farmers in ofi's sustainable supply chains remained below living income benchmarks [https://assets.ctfassets.net/yzlb24ni1fxz/701vWnha1u6CIE8r8kHL6b/a27daac5d82c54c512dcea36ef70d960/ofi_Cocoa_Compass_Report_FINAL_09_Dec_2025.pdf].

- **Regional disparities (2024):** In Brazil and Cameroon, 95 percent of farmers reached living income; Ecuador 81 percent; Indonesia 70 percent. In Ghana, Côte d'Ivoire, Nigeria, Papua New Guinea, and Uganda, the majority of farmers remained below living income thresholds [https://cocoaradar.com/ofi-hits-climate-milestone-as-women-and-children-move-to-the-centre-of-cocoa-sustainability/].

- **Caveat:** ofi explicitly acknowledged that "farmer incomes remain heavily influenced by external factors such as weather and global cocoa prices. Sharp price increases in 2024 contributed" to the living income gains, and that a future price drop "would have the opposite effect" [https://www.ofi.com/en-us/news-and-events/press-release/ofi-reaches-cocoa-greenhouse-gas-reduction-milestone].

### Comparative Summary of Farmer Income

| Metric | Barry Callebaut | Cargill | ofi |
|--------|-----------------|---------|-----|
| Baseline farmer income | ~€0.86/day (2013/14) | $72/season (PROCOCO Ghana) | Not clearly disclosed (2018) |
| Documented improvement | XOF 1.35M/farm (2021/22, highest since 2018/19); 31% income increase from LIRP | 29% income increase (Ghana CARE); income tripled to $239 (PROCOCO); $194 net increase from crop protection | 155,000 farmers at living income (2024); cocoa income doubled since 2023 |
| Living income achievement | ~45% of benchmark for study households | Targeting 25,000 households to close >50% of gap by 2025 | 45% of program farmers at living income (2024) |
| Primary driver | Yield improvement, farm services, price premiums | Training, coaching, financial access, crop protection | Market price increases, livelihood support programs |

---

## 5. EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) Compliance Responses

### 5.1 Regulatory Context

The EUDR (Regulation EU 2023/1115) requires that cocoa placed on the EU market be deforestation-free (no deforestation after December 31, 2020), legally produced, and traceable to the specific plot of land using geolocation polygons. Non-compliant volumes must be physically separated from compliant volumes; fines can reach 4 percent of annual turnover [https://environment.ec.europa.eu/document/download/744919a7-8650-4850-89ad-a597268cd69e_en].

### 5.2 Barry Callebaut

- **Traceability scale:** Mapped over 1.5 million cocoa farms by 2024/25, with 725,785 newly mapped plots that year [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/system/files/2025-11/Annual%20Social%20and%20Environmental%20Impact%20Report%202024_25.pdf]. Plot mapping coverage reached 85.6 percent of direct supply chain.
- **EUDR compliance claim:** "For volumes sourcing facilities in Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire, we have achieved 100% deforestation-free sourcing" within EUDR scope [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/sustainability/reporting/forever-chocolate-progress-report-202425].
- **Yield control system:** Implemented mechanisms using data on location, weather patterns, soil profile, and tree age to cap volumes procured per plot, embedded in first-mile traceability systems [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/about-us/media/news-stories/navigating-eudr-barry-callebauts-path-sustainable-excellence].
- **Customer commitment:** "In Europe, Barry Callebaut will only sell EUDR compliant products" with Due Diligence Statements provided for each batch [https://www.callebaut.com/sites/default/files/2025-01/EUDR%20Introduction%20for%20Distributors%20and%20Artisans%20-%20Callebaut.pdf].

### 5.3 Cargill

- **Implementation approach:** Initiated a two-phase EUDR project in early 2023, assessing readiness and implementing a management system for compliance [https://www.cargill.com/food-beverage/emea/news/almost-ready-for-eudr-inside-our-compliance-preparations].
- **First Mile Traceability (1ML):** In 2023/24, 101,735 farmers (78 percent of Promise supply chain farmers) delivered cocoa beans through the digital first mile traceability system. Within the Promise supply chain, 76.6 percent of beans were sourced through first mile digital traceability [https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/doc/1432271059750/2024-impact-report-cocoa.pdf].
- **Ghana vs. Côte d'Ivoire:** 100 percent farmer-to-factory traceability achieved in Ghana; 61 percent in Côte d'Ivoire (earlier data) [https://www.idhsustainabletrade.com/uploaded/2021/06/Case-Study-Cargill_FinalVersion.pdf].
- **Technology partnership:** Meridia for continuous verification and quality assurance of farm geodata; WRI Global Forest Watch Pro for satellite-based deforestation monitoring [https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/doc/1451373894196/2025-cargill-impact-report-cocoa.pdf].
- **Exclusion mechanism:** By crop year 2023/24, farmers were required to have all cocoa plots mapped and pass a deforestation risk area assessment to enter the Cocoa Promise supply chain, effectively excluding non-compliant volumes from the certified/direct stream [https://www.cargill.com/story/progress-protecting-forests-in-west-africa].

### 5.4 Olam/ofi

- **Head start claim:** Olam's 2024 Annual Report stated the company is "already well-placed to meet the obligations of the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) ahead of it coming into force" [https://www.olamgroup.com/content/dam/olamgroup/investor-relations/ir-library/annual-reports/annual-reports-pdfs/2024/olam_annual_report_2024.pdf].
- **100 percent direct traceability (2020):** Achieved 100 percent traceability of directly sourced cocoa across its global supply chain [https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/olam-cocoa-hits-100-percent-traceability-target-across-its-direct-global-supply-chain.html].
- **Technology stack:** OFIS records bag-level traceability, training activities, GPS-mapped farm data, feeding into the AtSource sustainability insights platform. Deploying granular deforestation mapping, child labor monitoring, and AI-powered carbon measurement tools [https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/ofi-cocoa-shares-how-transparency-and-traceability-are-driving-sustainability-impact-for-food-and-beverage-sector.html].
- **Indirect supply gap:** ofi's 2022 CFI progress report acknowledged a deforestation risk of just under 3 percent for farmer groups mapped within their direct Ghanaian supply chain. Specific measures for the indirect supply chain (approximately one-third of total volume) are not clearly outlined [https://mightyearth.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/RR_Cocoa_2_Update_4_18_24.pdf].

### 5.5 Comparative EUDR Readiness Assessment

| Dimension | Barry Callebaut | Cargill | ofi |
|-----------|----------------|---------|-----|
| Direct supply traceability to farm | 85.6% of plots mapped (2024/25); 100% for EU-bound direct volumes | 78% of Promise farmers via 1ML (2023/24); 100% in Ghana, 61% in Côte d'Ivoire (earlier data) | 100% of direct supply chain (since 2020) |
| Total farms mapped | 1.5 million+ (2024/25) | 101,735 via 1ML (2023/24) | 325,000+ using OFIS technology |
| Deforestation-free claim | 100% for Ghana/Côte d'Ivoire direct-to-facility volumes | Required for Cocoa Promise entry by 2023/24 | 100% deforestation monitoring across direct chain (2020) |
| Technology partners | Strategic partners for digital infrastructure | Meridia (geodata verification), WRI Global Forest Watch | OFIS, AtSource, remote sensing |
| Disclosed non-compliant volume exclusions | Not quantified publicly | Not quantified publicly | ~3% deforestation risk acknowledged in direct Ghanaian supply chain |

**Key uncertainty:** None of the three companies has publicly disclosed specific tonnage or percentage of non-compliant volumes excluded from their supply chains as a result of EUDR due diligence. This represents a significant transparency gap, particularly given that indirect supply chains (representing 33–60 percent of volumes depending on company) remain substantially untraceable.

---

## 6. Competitive Positioning in Premium Chocolate Manufacturing Contracts

### 6.1 Barry Callebaut

Barry Callebaut holds the strongest position in industrial chocolate outsourcing contracts:

- **Mondelēz International:** Relationship dates to a 2010 long-term global master supply agreement, the company's largest outsourcing deal at the time. Extended with acquisition of Mondelēz's Halle, Belgium chocolate production facility, adding 30,000 tonnes of annual liquid chocolate supply [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/group/media/news-stories/barry-callebaut-extend-strategic-supply-partnership-mondelez-international]. Mondelēz's 2021 supplier list showed Barry Callebaut as its largest cocoa supplier in Côte d'Ivoire with 27 cooperatives, followed by Olam (16), Cargill (13), and ECOM (8) [https://aidenvironment.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/Compliance-Checker_Mondelez_Profile_By-AidEnvironment.pdf]. Mondelēz's Cocoa Life program covered 91 percent of cocoa volume by end of 2024 [https://www.cocoalife.org/en/progress/mdlz-2024-snacking-made-right-report/].

- **Hershey:** Extended strategic supply partnership (originally signed 2007) for liquid chocolate and finished products across the US, Canada, and Mexico [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/about-us/media/news-stories/hershey-and-barry-callebaut-extend-strategic-supply-agreement].

- **Grupo Bimbo:** Extended global strategic supply agreement covering Mexico, Central America, the US, Canada, and Uruguay [https://www.barry-callebaut.com/en/about-us/media/news-stories/grupo-bimbo-and-barry-callebaut-extend-strategic-supply-agreement].

- **EUDR competitive advantage:** Barry Callebaut's early investment in farm mapping (1.5 million+ farms), its commitment to sell only EUDR-compliant products in Europe, and its provision of DDS documentation position it as the most EUDR-ready supplier for premium manufacturers requiring compliant cocoa.

### 6.2 Cargill

- **Mondelēz:** Key Cocoa Life partner in both Côte d'Ivoire and Indonesia. In Côte d'Ivoire, Mondelēz works with Cargill and NGO Care [https://www.confectionerynews.com/Article/2015/11/03/Mondelez-and-Barry-Callebaut-team-on-cocoa-sustainability/]. Cargill supplies 13 cooperatives in Mondelēz's Côte d'Ivoire supply chain.

- **Nestlé:** Key partner in the Income Accelerator Program (IAP) since its inception, supporting cash transfer models and income diversification [https://www.cargill.com/sustainability/doc/1432271059750/2024-impact-report-cocoa.pdf].

- **Fully segregated cocoa:** Offers fully segregated cocoa ingredients traced from farm to facility, directly addressing EUDR's segregation requirements and premium brand demands.

- **Competitive challenge:** Cargill's higher proportion of indirect sourcing (40 percent globally, potentially up to two-thirds per some assessments) creates a competitive vulnerability for EUDR compliance, particularly if premium manufacturers require full traceability. The company's acknowledged challenges with geolocation data across smallholder networks may limit its ability to compete with Barry Callebaut for the most stringent EUDR-compliant contracts in the short term.

### 6.3 Olam/ofi

- **Mondelēz:** Partnered with Mondelēz in April 2021 to create the "world's single largest sustainable commercial cocoa farm" in Indonesia, combining Cocoa Life and Cocoa Compass frameworks [https://ir.mondelezinternational.com/news-releases/news-release-details/mondelez-international-and-olam-food-ingredients-announce]. ofi supplies 16 cooperatives in Mondelēz's Côte d'Ivoire supply chain.

- **Customer base:** ofi supplies over 2,000 customers globally, offering AtSource as a sustainability insights platform [https://www.olamgroup.com/news/all-news/press-release/olam-food-ingredients-reaches-cocoa-compass-targets-for-100-percent-child-labour-and-deforestation-monitoring.html].

- **EUDR positioning:** ofi's early achievement of 100 percent direct supply chain traceability (2020) and its comprehensive OFIS/AtSource technology platform provide a strong competitive foundation. The company's 19 percent market share of all cocoa produced in Ghana in 2019/20 demonstrates deep upstream integration [https://mightyearth.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/RR_Cocoa_2_Update_4_18_24.pdf].

- **Competitive challenge:** ofi's lack of clarity around the direct/indirect split and its limited disclosure of indirect supply chain traceability measures create uncertainty for customers evaluating EUDR risk. The company's processing footprint in West Africa is smaller than Barry Callebaut's and Cargill's, potentially limiting its capacity for large-scale origin-processed premium ingredients.

### 6.4 Premium Manufacturer Sourcing Strategies

**Lindt & Sprüngli:** One of the few chocolate manufacturers producing bean-to-bar, sourcing cocoa beans from five origin countries and processing its own cocoa mass. Since 2008, the company has operated its own Lindt & Sprüngli Farming Program built on Rainforest Alliance certification. By 2025, 100 percent of cocoa products were sourced through the Farming Program or other responsible sourcing standards [https://www.farming-program.com/en/about-the-farming-program]. Lindt is listed as a Cocoa & Forests Initiative (CFI) signatory alongside the three processors.

**Ferrero:** Sources cocoa through Cocoa Horizons, Rainforest Alliance, and Fairtrade certifications. Approximately 85 percent of Ferrero's cocoa comes from dedicated farmer groups, with 99 percent through sustainability standards. Ferrero processes approximately 70 percent of its cocoa beans in its own plants, requiring traceable, segregated supply chains that align with Barry Callebaut's and ofi's capabilities.

**Mondelēz:** Cocoa Life program, 91 percent of cocoa volume through program (2024), $1 billion investment by 2030. Sources from Barry Callebaut (27 cooperatives in Côte d'Ivoire), Olam (16), Cargill (13), ECOM (8). This multi-supplier strategy reflects Mondelēz's risk diversification approach, but also means each processor must compete for a share of one of the world's largest sustainable cocoa commitments.

---

## 7. Key Uncertainties and Data Gaps

1. **Non-compliant volume exclusions:** Despite EUDR requirements for segregation of compliant and non-compliant cocoa, no company has publicly disclosed specific volumes of cocoa excluded from European supply chains on non-compliance grounds. This will be a critical metric to track post-EUDR enforcement.

2. **Indirect sourcing traceability:** None of the three companies has achieved full traceability of their indirect supply chains. Published traceability figures overwhelmingly cover direct supply chains only. This remains the most significant "blind spot" for EUDR compliance [https://trase.earth/insights/cote-d-ivoire-cocoa-exports-and-deforestation-2026].

3. **Farmer income data limitations:** Income figures reported by all three companies are subject to methodological variations (poverty benchmarks, household size assumptions, attribution of market price effects vs. program interventions). Independent verification remains limited. The sharp 2023–2024 cocoa price increase substantially boosted farmer income metrics, and all three companies acknowledge that future price drops could reverse these gains.

4. **Contract specifics with premium manufacturers:** Long-term outsourcing agreements between processors and chocolate manufacturers (Lindt, Ferrero, Mondelēz) are typically confidential. Published evidence relies on press releases and sustainability program partnership announcements rather than contractual terms.

5. **ofi Ghana-specific capex 2018–2024:** Specific capital expenditure figures for ofi's Ghana operations during this period were not publicly itemized in available sources; most ofi capex disclosures cover San Pedro (Côte d'Ivoire) and Indonesian BT Cocoa acquisition.

---

## 8. Strategic Assessment

**Barry Callebaut** has emerged as the EUDR compliance leader, converting regulatory burden into competitive advantage. Its aggressive shift to direct cooperative sourcing (86 percent in Côte d'Ivoire by 2024), 1.5 million mapped farms, and 100 percent deforestation-free claims for key origin volumes make it the preferred partner for manufacturers like Mondelēz that need outsourced, compliant chocolate volumes at scale. Its FY 2023/24 revenue rose 28.1 percent in local currencies to CHF 10.4 billion, partly capturing bean-price inflation through origin processing, though it also absorbed significant working-capital risk (free cash flow of CHF −2,330.7 million in FY 2023/24).

**Cargill** combines large-scale origin processing ($113 million invested in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana) with robust cooperative capacity-building. Its 60:40 direct-to-indirect split leaves a larger compliance gap than Barry Callebaut's. Cargill's strength lies in data-driven living income partnerships and its Nestlé IAP collaboration, positioning it well for contracts where social impact metrics are prioritized alongside traceability.

**Olam/ofi** possesses unmatched African origin origination and an enviable customer list including Lindt, Ferrero, and Mondelēz. Its early achievement of 100 percent direct-chain traceability (2020) and AtSource transparency platform are strong assets. Yet its group-level indirect sourcing exposure and later-starting verification for uncertified suppliers mean it faces higher EUDR execution risk. The delay of its ofi IPO may constrain capital available to close these gaps as quickly as Barry Callebaut has.

**Quantitative synthesis:** Over the 2018–2024 period, the three companies collectively invested well over $400 million in West African processing capacity, mapped hundreds of thousands of farms, and reported lifting between 30,000 and 557,000 farmers above poverty or living income benchmarks. However, the industry still faces a structural traceability gap: as of 2024, an estimated 52 percent of Ivorian cocoa exports remained hidden from public traceability due to indirect sourcing, and average indirect-chain traceability across traders stood at just 22 percent. The processor that closes this indirect-supply gap first—currently Barry Callebaut based on disclosed data—will likely capture disproportionate share in the premium and EUDR-compliant contract market through 2025 and beyond.
